items.q: What is the function of dystopian fiction?
items.correct: To critique tendencies in the writer's own society by extrapolating them into a nightmarish future — dystopias hold up a warning mirror to the present.
items.misconception: Dystopian fiction is just storytelling set in scary futures.
items.random_wrong: Dystopian fiction predicts what will actually happen in 100 years.
items.why: Dystopias are about the present, not the future. 1984 critiques 1948; The Handmaid's Tale critiques 1980s America. Students miss the satirical function and read them as prophecy.

items.correct: Their account is undermined within the text by ignorance, bias, self-deception or deceit, so the reader must read against what they say.
items.misconception: An unreliable narrator is one who tells lies in the story.
items.random_wrong: An unreliable narrator is when the author makes mistakes.
items.why: Unreliability is structural — the text gives signals the narrator is wrong (e.g. internal contradictions). Lying intentionally is one kind, but blind spots and self-deception count too.

items.correct: When a sentence or phrase runs over from one line of poetry to the next without a punctuated pause, creating flow or surprise across the line break.
items.misconception: Enjambment is when a poem has very long lines.
items.random_wrong: Enjambment is when a poem uses no punctuation at all.
items.why: Enjambment is about syntax crossing the line break, not line length or punctuation in general. Students miss that it is about the relationship between grammar and lineation.

items.q: Who wrote Seven Types of Ambiguity?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: William Empson. “I A Richards” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the E in PEEL stand for?
items.why: It's Evidence. “Example” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many types of ambiguity does Empson identify?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
